data.title: Globant Is A Great Business, But There's Not Enough Value To Buy
data.description:
Globant (NYSE:GLOB) stock is not cheap, and I expect that its valuation multiples will contract slightly in the next 12 months. That said, I admire the company's clever operating model (including nearshore development) and strong positioning amid high digital transformation demand. Based on my growth estimates for the company and its present valuation, the stock price is likely to be approximately equal to the present levels in a year.

Operational Analysis

Globant is a technology services provider focusing on a range of key areas, including custom software development and IT services, digital and interactive experiences, cloud services, artificial intelligence and data analytics, and industry-specific expertise.

The company's AI Reinvention Network is particularly noteworthy as a core future growth driver-it is designed to accelerate AI adoption across various industries, including automotive, healthcare, finance, and more. Moreover, the company has introduced AI Agents into the software development lifecycle, which enhances productivity by automating tasks throughout the development process.

I think management is correct in focusing on AI as one of its core competencies-the AI market is likely to be one of the largest in the world, based on my analysis. Globant has significantly increased its AI talent pool by hiring data scientists and AI engineers, and I expect that as the company continues to consolidate its talent and position here, it will continue to prove highly accretive to the company.

Globant's focus on digital transformation services means it is very well-positioned as trends in digital modernization continue to expand. The company has also developed a very strong expertise in cloud migration and management, allowing it to capitalize on the current growth opportunity in cloud services. Moreover, management has established partnerships with Big Tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), allowing Globant to integrate cutting-edge technologies into its solutions, making its digital transformation services far more comprehensive.

There's a lot to be bullish on here; however, as with any high-growth industry, the digital transformation market is highly saturated. That being said, it is also likely big enough to support many larger and smaller players for the foreseeable future.

In terms of direct competitors of a similar size, Globant competes with Endava (DAVA), EPAM Systems (EPAM), and ASGN Incorporated (ASGN). Of course, these are not the only companies that Globant faces competition risk from; it also competes with the much more dominant Accenture (ACN), Infosys (INFY), and Cognizant (CTSH).

Valuation & Financial Analysis

Globant is currently the most highly valued company based on EV to EBITDA of its similar-sized peers that I have identified. However, it is also deservedly so based on its markedly higher EBITDA growth. The company's EBITDA margin of 15% is over 50% higher than the sector median, so I think management should really be commended for successful profitability at the operational level. That being said, its gross margin is 28.3% lower than the sector median. Therefore, if management can find ways to reduce the cost of goods sold, it could become dominantly stronger than its similar-sized peers (it currently has the highest gross margin already). Globant also currently has a higher gross margin by approximately three percentage points than Accenture.

The company's next earnings results are on 15 November, and the consensus estimate indicates a 9.76% year-over-year growth in its normalized EPS. Moreover, the consensus revenue estimate is $614.58 million, which is a quarter-over-quarter growth of 4.6% from Q2's revenue of $587.46 million.

Given that the company is experiencing strong tailwinds from its AI-related revenues at the moment, I find it highly unlikely that the company will underperform the consensus estimates, which are approximately in the middle of management's guidance. Furthermore, the company utilizes a nearshore development model through its Latin American offices, offering cost-effective services while maintaining cultural and time zone compatibility with North American clients; I expect this is likely to substantially support the company's growth in 2025, which is likely to be a stronger year due to lower interest rates supporting digital transformation spending.

Given my outlook on the company's strong operational positioning at present, I estimate that the company will achieve a normalized EPS of $1.64 and revenue of $615 million for the quarter.

However, with the company's forward P/E non-GAAP ratio currently a 29% contraction from its five-year average but its forward diluted EPS growth a 45% contraction from its five-year average, I think there is some valid cause for concern here from an earning-based valuation standpoint. This weakness in its valuation is further reinforced by the fact that it has a forward P/S ratio that is a 36% contraction from its five-year average but forward revenue growth that is a 42% contraction from its five-year average.

Based on this, I estimate that the company's P/E non-GAAP ratio will contract over the next 12 months-I find it unlikely that the stock's valuation multiples will decline in direct correlation to its financial growth rate contractions because I think the market holds strong sentiment for this company's future prospects, particularly related to its position in AI. Therefore, in a base case, a P/E non-GAAP ratio contraction to 30 over the next 12 months seems reasonable to me. I estimate that the company will achieve approximately a 16.5% normalized EPS growth rate for FY25, arriving at a normalized EPS of approximately $7.46 for the full year. Therefore, my 12-month price target for the stock is approximately $223.80. This is equal to the current price. Therefore, my rating is a Hold.

Risk Analysis

Especially at the moment with heightened AI demand, Globant's revenue is sensitive to fluctuations in client spending. I expect that as major AI companies finalize their initial AI infrastructure build-outs, the initial demand for AI services will begin to slow down following this. There is the possibility that we will see a contraction in revenues for many companies that initially benefitted from the high initial demand for AI. The current high demand for AI is not something I expect to stay constant (although I do expect it to scale in cycles over time as new technology proficiencies and macroeconomic conditions facilitate this). Therefore, I think any company investing heavily in generating revenue from AI demand should expect a period of protracted decline (unless revenue is sourced as recurring, but even then, one-off services will likely reduce significantly). To put it more simply, I find AI spending to be highly susceptible to recession.

Moreover, while AI and robotics markets have a very strong overlap, there are distinct differences in the expertise of talent required in both fields. Therefore, without proper foresight and preparation, management could easily fail to develop the right employee base to secure high-revenue contracts in robotics transformations. Management will want to be strategic in its timing here because too heavy an emphasis on robotics too early on will be negative and impact the company's margins significantly.

In keeping with the above chart, management needs to anticipate when we are near a major upcycle in robotics to prepare its infrastructure in advance. Another way management could do this (and I believe it has shown evidence of this strategy already with its emphasis on AI) is to build an agile culture with the necessary cohorts that can then pivot to robotics quickly when the demand picks up-predicting a major upcycle is incredibly difficult.

Conclusion

I am bullish on Globant operationally, but the reality is that not every great business can deliver alpha. Therefore, my rating must be a Hold, even though I admire management and the positioning of its company cost-effectively in a very high-growth field. I expect that with the right preparedness, the company will continue to capitalize on trends in modern digitalization for years to come. However, at its current valuation, investors would be better off looking elsewhere for high returns, especially as Globant does not pay a dividend at this time.

data.title: NLR: Nuclear Boom Doesn't Trickle Down Much To Utility Shareholders
data.description:
The VanEck Uranium and Nuclear Energy ETF (NYSEARCA:NLR) is a pretty broad utilities ETF focused on companies with significant generation from nuclear capacity, but also some stocks with positive exposure to Uranium prices.

Nuclear in general has seen a bit of a revival, according to our broader thesis on the theme some years ago, with the added (debatable) benefit of the AI revolution further amping demand with nice economics from long-dated PPA agreements on the very high operating leverage nuclear assets across the industry. While nuclear generators benefit somewhat from PPA wins which is nice and may justify keeping these assets in use longer, there are still mitigating factors in that they are expensive to maintain beyond their intended life even if there are maybe some discounting effect benefits from doing so and of course the opportunity to earn continued intermediate income without needing to start a new generation project. The dwindled human capital and expertise in developing and maintaining nuclear assets hasn't helped either, with several generations having come into a world where it was being assumed that nuclear was on the way out.

Nuclear is great, and altogether has been an underutilised quantum-leap technology produced by a rare gathering of the Western geniuses of the 20th century. However, we don't generally see much incremental upside from the recent headlines for utilities stocks in connection with AI training power. PPA agreements are typically long-dated so there's nothing special about the length of these agreements, and while AI is a secular demand sink for power, electricity prices aren't going off the charts on AI's account, although they are generally higher now than 5 years ago.

In all, a market to develop nuclear assets doesn't necessarily mean outstanding incremental returns for the utility companies which are saddled with big responsibilities in generating nuclear energy and are by regulatory design not set up for extraordinary returns. In fact, IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) reported negative NPVs for developing and only marginally better but still negative NPVs for extended-life models, from before the costs of capital rose after 2019. We don't really see obvious value creation for the nuclear utilities other than the markets maybe having more of a taste for nuclear exposure now than they used to, meaning less idiosyncratic discounts, and in limited cases where restarts are happening of nuclear plants. But this is speculative. As such, while small-scale reactor companies like Oklo (OKLO) and particularly modular reactor stocks might be trendy and interesting right now, and maybe stocks with more explicit Uranium spot price exposure as Uranium demand should be growing, we aren't particularly moved to go for NLR just because nuclear is less taboo now, and would probably make a more specific bet within nuclear and not on the utilities.

NLR Quick Breakdown

The NLR portfolio is composed substantially of straightforward utility companies in the US, some spun off from larger ones, containing a nuclear skew in their generating portfolios. These are utility companies, so the name of the game is the same as any other utility which is purchase price agreement wins to keep assets properly utilised and the hope of high electricity prices (for those that don't do CCGT). This is how all utility companies basically function, either establishing PPAs for assets in development (to finance asset development securely) or rolling over PPAs to keep current generating assets highly utilised. These agreements are often with corporate customers depending on the structure of the electricity markets.

The big headlines lately have been that Google (GOOG) has entered into agreements with small-scale reactor producers in order to deal with the power demand for training AI at their data centers. We understand why the small-scale reactor class of nuclear stocks would go up on account of new and important business, but ETFs like the NLR have also gone up significantly. There have been other headlines surging utility companies in any instance where they are named as having signed PPA agreements with AI-oriented companies.

Considerations

We have some reasons to think the run-up of NLR may not be entirely sensible. Firstly, some utility companies have increased on announcements of AI-related PPA agreements. Constellation (CEG) energy has picked up meaningfully. Electricity prices haven't made any meaningful movements sequentially, but the announcement that Microsoft (MSFT) has entered into a 20-year PPA CEG caused some stock action. In the case of Constellation Energy, the excitement might be coming from the fact that the nuclear assets in question are going to be restarted after being put down before COVID-19, and that Microsoft might be paying a premium in the PPA. However, that same asset was shut down for economic reasons in 2019, reflecting the challenges in these assets. Electricity prices are quite a bit higher than they were in 2019, so it is possibly a positive outcome ultimately as that asset returns to profits. Also, Cameco (CCJ) within NLR is restarting some of its Uranium operations that were also paused due to a weak Uranium market, but they're not a utility, although it's a positive revision for the company. However, going back to the nuclear generating companies, not that many restarts are in store most likely and consistent with that investors should generally keep the following in mind:

  • PPA agreements of 20-year length are entirely within the norm, so there shouldn't be a big incremental change in the certainty profile of the PPA portfolio at the company from getting a new deal like that.
  • Demand for nuclear specifically as a green and high-capacity factor energy source may mean that nuclear assets slated for decommissioning may not be decommissioned on their original schedule, and their lives instead extended. Many assets have received extensions in the name of energy security post Ukraine war. However, this should not necessarily mean much incremental benefit for the company's shareholders that steward these assets. Firstly, speculation around these issues tend to not impact nuclear-levered utility stocks in Europe, which is usually a more rational and less algorithmically driven market due to non-uniform disclosure systems compared to the US where everything ends up on SEC.gov. That is an important sign that the economic benefits are debatable.
  • Secondly, this is consistent with the cost to extend the life of a nuclear asset by 10 years which tends to be as much as the decommissioning cost (both in the range of $500-800 million). Yes, staving off decommissioning comes with some positive discounting effects, and the asset continues to generate income so it has a positive incremental NPV effect, but it is still a major investment to keep nuclear running especially as talent for developing and maintaining nuclear, as well as component supply, has been underinvested for some decades now. Also, a lot of the life extension investment is upfront. We think a positive thing could be the longer the life of the asset the more chance for uprate investments, but these investments are probably expensive enough too where the incremental shareholder value creation from making them is not enough to justify price action, and the uprate matter doesn't appear to be part of the established narrative.

Conclusions

We like nuclear, and think utilising it is probably a good idea since it's already there. But for the utility company business model, even those with more nuclear skew, we don't see anything in the headlines around AI PPA agreements that would uniquely benefit them over PPA agreements with other commercial customers. As said, we also think that it is highly debatable whether life extensions of nuclear assets actually benefits the shareholders of the utility companies that have to steward these assets. New asset development would be even more questionable, although no one is talking about that yet. In the case of a restart, this might be a positive revision, but could still mean negative incremental NPVs as decommissioning costs are inevitable. Also, opportunities for worthwhile asset restarts are unclear and likely limited. While electricity prices are generally higher than they were at the time of these reports in 2019, so are capital costs. LCOEs, or the net present cost of generating electricity used for comparing generation methods, were barely changing in the extended models.

Also, if nuclear assets continue on much longer than expected, this might also impact electricity prices negatively as the expected amount of future supply is revised upwards in negotiations for PPAs. Perhaps the nuclear discount is shrinking, and to the extent that NLR is exposed to Uranium spot prices the decent performance there would also help NLR performance, but other than that and maybe the more particular instances like CEG which is restarting a mothballed asset, we wouldn't expect that a nuclear boom would be especially good for shareholders, and we aren't convinced that it explains or justifies the NLR's 42% 1-year growth which comes down to performance from other factors in the NLR's relatively concentrated portfolio. We wouldn't make a utility play on nuclear so NLR isn't our favourite.

data.title: AbbVie: Strong Dividends But Humira Disappoints
data.description:
Healthcare is both defensive and a mean reversion case

After writing a recent article on Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), I had some requests in the comment section to cover AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV). This is a stock I have owned for a while that was purchased a few years ago as part of a "Magic Formula" 30 stock buy and hold strategy that I implement for a part of my portfolio.

The company remains on the Magic Formula screener as a good value, starting at stocks with a market cap of $80 billion or larger. However, the screener is not flawless and there are some anomalies that make free cash flow larger than the valuation metric I used, "owner earnings". The main difference is the line item "write down and restructuring costs" which was recorded on the cash flow statement at nearly $5 billion on a trailing twelve-month basis is not a sustainable add-back to cash from operations. Owner earnings only utilize Depreciation and Amortization as an add-back.

AbbVie is a decent deal based on the dividend, but new CEO Robert Michael who took over for Richard Gonzalez, an internal promotion from Michael's COO role prior to Gonzalez's retirement, made it seem like single-digit growth is about the best the company has in store for the next few years. More in a bit, but the Humira patent expiration in 2023 also presents a bigger problem than I think the new CEO's first earnings call opined. I own the stock and will continue to, but the stock is currently a hold in my opinion until either the stock grows into its valuation or the price comes down.

Current story and trends

AbbVie is one of the pure pharma plays in healthcare that is solely reliant on its drug sales and in-organic acquisitions of other drug companies to grow top and bottom lines. They are dissimilar to a company like Johnson & Johnson, which also has a med tech division and is more in line with competitors like Amgen (AMGN) or Pfizer (PFE).

The main area where AbbVie differs from other pharmaceutical companies is that they have one of the highest exposures to "immunology" versus oncology drugs, at more than 50% revenue exposure. Think of things like psoriasis, Crohn's disease and other drugs related to autoimmune disorders.

Humira exclusivity has ended

An excellent 2023 piece from Healio describes just how big of a deal Humira is:

In 2021, Humira — the blockbuster biologic that has for years been the highest grossing drug in the world — accomplished something that no drug had previously achieved when its global revenues topped $20 billion.

Through both biosimilar competition and an ending of exclusivity in 2023, the Humira boom is over. Revenues have started to deteriorate, and the ending of this blockbuster drug's exclusivity was probably a factor in the previous CEO stepping down at the top of the mountain. It didn't get much better for the company than the 10-year run up to 2023 which saw revenues absolutely boom.

Both revenue and gross profit more than doubled during these 10 years, and the new CEO has big shoes to fill to keep investors happy for the next 10.

Revenue exposure

All numbers in millions courtesy of ABBVIE most recent 10-Q:

SEGMENT REVENUE PERCENT
Immunology 6971 56.5%
Oncology 1506 12.2%
Aesthetics 1390 11.27%
Neuroscience 1931 15.65%
Eye care 533 4.32%
Total from major segments 12331

This is the most recent revenue exposure for AbbVie from their most recent quarter's 10-Q covering not all of their revenue, but the revenue that covers their wholly owned drugs. We can see that AbbVie is a heavy play on immunology. I personally think this is going to be a growing industry as someone who suffers from food allergies. The amount of specialized foods that now exist to help allergy sufferers with autoimmune disorders is a great indication that this trajectory is only going one way.

With Rheumatoid Arthritis being one of the main presenting issues from autoimmune disorders along with intestinal and dermatological, Humira was definitely a "catch-all" for this segment.

IMMUNOLOGY SEGMENT Revenue Percent of segment
Humira 2814 41.20%
Skyrizi 2727 40.90%
Rinvoq 1430 17.70%
total 6971

With the self-proclaimed "degradation" of Humira earnings on the most recent quarter's earnings call, Skyrizi is coming up the back end in the immunology segment and will soon become AbbVie's leading drug. This drug goes beyond mere Rheumatoid Arthritis and gets into being a cytokine disruptor aimed at alleviating a whole host of autoimmune issues including plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, Crohn's Disease, and Ulcerative Colitis.

With the primary patent for Skyrizi not expiring until 2033, this will be the primary cash cow for the company until further notice, in my opinion.

Upcoming Earnings

With earnings set to report October 30th, there have been 10 upward revisions since the last earnings report, where the new CEO guided higher on the call. I did find it odd that he expressed such confidence in how well everything was going after a miss on the bottom line.

Keep in mind, in comparison to last year, Humira revenue has declined 31.6% on a 3-month basis and 35.4% on a 6-month basis. If this keeps up and other drugs don't pick up the slack, it will be difficult to keep growing at high single digits as guided.

The company has already started to look at earnings growth "ex Humira", which means they are putting that top revenue generator on a perpetual negative growth train to hades.

Measuring reflexive trends

10-year trend

On a ten-year basis, the reflexive trends for long-term holders have not been horrible, with free cash flow growing well ahead of price growth. However, we see obvious trends in chart lines dipping downward starting in 2023 for revenue, normalized EPS, and free cash flow. Let's zoom in closer at the 5-year level.

5-year trend

On a 5-year comparison, we now see all major profitability metrics [revenue, free cash flow and normalized EPS] dipping well below price growth during this period. For new buyers, the current trend might not be your friend.

Valuation

AbbVie is a slow grower. Thus, this company needs to be weighed against its current "cheapness" using the risk-free rate.

In this model, I use the classic Warren Buffett "Owner Earnings" model. Similar to discounted cash flow, except this takes into account taxes, interest and stock-based compensation. This model uses GAAP net income as it's base profitability metric and then adds back depreciation and amortization. Finally, TTM CAPEX is deducted.

This is all discounted by the risk-free rate using the 10-year Treasury. Buffett argued that if a company could predictably grow earnings from the point of entry into the future, then an investment in the evaluated stock would be better than an investment in a risk-free alternative like the 10-year Treasury since that is a fixed return versus a growing one for said company. Currently, the risk-free rate sits around 4.2%, and I will use this as my discount rate.

In the case of AbbVie on a trailing twelve-month basis, we have the following:

All numbers in millions courtesy of Seeking Alpha:

  • Net Income: $5,339
  • Depreciation and Amortization: $8,516
  • TTM CAPEX:$858
  • Equation: [$5,339 + $8,516]-$858= $12,997 "Owner Earnings"
  • $12,997/4.2%= $309,452 fair market cap.
  • Divided by shares outstanding [1,766.3] = $175.19 fair price.

With consensus estimated earnings guidance slated at 11% growth in EPS from FY 2024 to FY 2025, that would put the fair price at $194.46 if all other items in this model remained constant [CAPEX requirements and depreciation and amortization]. That would make AbbVie slightly undervalued based on next year's earnings, but I'd rather see the profitability trend lines tick positive before I take the chance to buy more shares with such a thin margin of safety.

The dividend

As a percentage of free cash flow per share:

  • TTM FCF/share= $10.05
  • FCF payout ratio= 61%

This is one of the main items that makes this stock popular, it pays a really nice dividend with growth for 11 years and a moderate payout ratio.

The dividend is actually second best on this list of peers in my opinion as AbbVie provides a 3.28% yield which is still covered on a GAAP earnings basis. Gilead Sciences (GILD) would be the top yield at 3.55% and still covered.

Even though the dividend looks safe for now, they are going through a write-down phase with Humira that is freeing up some free cash flow with the write-offs but creating more moving parts, making it more difficult to predict future free cash flow.

Balance sheet

This balance sheet is a bit of a mixed bag observing my 3 favorite metrics, shares outstanding, long-term debt and cash and short-term investments.

Share count may tend to fluctuate in big pharma names as acquisitions come into the pipeline, which may be purchased with shares if advantageous due to share price appreciation.

Long-term debt has dipped a bit and cash has leveled out since those share issuances. We can clearly see the correlation between cash and shares during that early 2020s period.

Nothing to write home about here, not a particularly strong or weak balance sheet, with LT debt about 5 X cash and only about 2 turns greater than the company's $26.12 Billion TTM EBITDA.

Share buybacks would be welcome here to start getting the float back on a downward trajectory, or else AbbVie might start creating its own headwinds to beating analyst expectations for EPS.

Catalysts

Cerevel was the most recent big acquisition closing in August, here are some details for the bolt on outlook:

AbbVie's acquisition of Cerevel strengthens our foundation in neuroscience and positions us to deliver sustainable long-term performance into the next decade and beyond," said Robert A. Michael, chief executive officer, AbbVie. "Our new Cerevel colleagues share our commitment to deliver meaningful change for patients living with neurological and psychiatric conditions. We are excited to welcome the talented Cerevel team to AbbVie."

There are multiple programs in Cerevel's pipeline across several neurological and psychiatric conditions such as schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease and mood disorders, where there continues to be significant unmet need for patients. Cerevel's pipeline is highly complementary to AbbVie's existing neuroscience portfolio and the completion of the acquisition is an important step forward to delivering new and better tolerated therapies.

Keep in mind, Cerevel is a pre-revenue mid-stage drug developer that AbbVie can now take to market, but the end market is in no way a proven top or bottom line grower.

In Cerevel's most recent 10Q, they lay out the R&D costs for their drug lineup, listed in thousands. This is the final 10Q before going private and becoming part of AbbVie.

The total R&D cash burn before absorption stood at over $100 million.

The other growth areas, as focused on by CEO Robert Michael, were the cross-selling of the different immunology drugs. From my understanding, many of these immunology drugs can be prescribed for different autoimmune disorders. Having the drugs prescribed for new autoimmune disorders that they were not previously prescribed for are new organic growth areas.

Risks and summary

We have a new CEO after an amazing run with Humira, and a doubling in top-line revenue over the last decade.

Now the company seems to be relying on a bump in other immunology drugs entering new autoimmune prescriptions combined with a growth bump in oncology and growth in neuroscience. The company has goals and a clear trajectory of what they want to do, but it could be a long road and the current growth rate lines in all profitability metrics are pointing in the wrong direction.

The stock also seems overvalued on an owner-earnings model up against this year's earnings and moderately undervalued up against next year's earnings if they hit analysts' predicted growth rates. This is a hold for the nice covered dividend, but not cheap enough for a buy rating based on profitability trends.

data.title: Tackle Fear And Uncertainties With Big Recurrent Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

It's that time of the year again. Halloween decorations are being put up, movie trailers for scary movies are being released, and marathons of old scary movies are airing on TV. Some people love the feeling of being scared, and October provides us with many options to seek that feeling. You can watch a scary movie, go on a haunted hay ride, visit haunted houses, or get lost in a corn maze.

One place none of us wants to be scared is our portfolios. Yet, in recent weeks, we've been getting an increase in questions within our Investing Group and comments on our public articles–questions that tell me people are scared.

  • What about the ILA port strike?
  • Will war escalate in Israel?
  • What about Hurricane Milton?
  • Who will win the Presidential race? People might not agree which outcome is scary, but many people believe that one of the outcomes is.
  • What about a recession?
  • What about a Black Swan?

Based on the questions we've been receiving, people are scared. Their first reaction?

Fear Is Natural

First, let me be clear: if you have these types of fears, there is absolutely nothing wrong with that. Fear is a natural part of investing. After all, we are managing portfolios that represent a substantial portion of our net worth.

It doesn't matter whether you have thousands, tens of thousands, hundreds of thousands, or millions; we all share something in common. The amounts in our portfolios represent a very substantial portion of our life savings. It is an amount we sacrificed to build up for years, passing on all the various things we could have spent that money on. It is very likely that during your working years, your peers were living grander lifestyles. They were living in nicer houses, driving nicer cars, taking more lavish vacations, etc. Many who earned a similar income to yourself, chose to spend instead of save. You reading this article tells me that whatever your income was, you decided not to spend it all. You decided to invest in your future, and that is a sacrifice.

So, when you are looking at your nest egg that you worked so hard to build, it is perfectly natural to fear that something might happen to it. There is absolutely nothing wrong with having these emotions. However, we do need to identify when these emotions might be having an adverse impact on our decisions. For example, a parent is going to be a bit fearful the first time their 16-year-old drives off in the car alone, but it wouldn't be healthy if they decided that their child should never learn how to drive to avoid that feeling.

Uncertainty Is Inevitable

As investors, we frequently face the reality that we don't know everything. Publicly traded companies report earnings once every three months. While we might get glimpses of information through various press releases or news reports, this information is frequently incomplete. We only get the full picture when the company files a 10-K or 10-Q. As a result, we have to fill in the gaps with estimations and educated guesses.

It is obvious that we don't know the future, but it is also important to keep in mind that we often don't have perfect information about what is happening right now.

All of your investment decisions will be made with a certain degree of uncertainty.

Doubt Is Healthy, To A Point

When you mix fear with the uncertainty that is inherent in investing, you get doubt. You start second-guessing whether you made the right investments. You will start to doubt whether you made a smart decision by deciding to buy or sell.

This is actually very healthy. Every once in a while, it is good to go through your portfolio and remind yourself why you bought what you did. This is one reason why we make it a point to routinely cover earnings. We want to take a fresh look at the stocks we own and decide if they are still good investments.

You should be checking in on your portfolio holdings occasionally, to see if things are going as you expect and consider making changes if it is appropriate.

On the other hand, if you are making frequent adjustments in your portfolio, buying, and selling the same stock multiple times, that might indicate an unhealthy level of doubt. When you make an investment, it isn't realistic to expect that the price is only going to go up. Stock prices are volatile. When we invest in publicly traded stocks, we should expect prices to be volatile. We shouldn't worry about it because it is natural and expected.

The Income Method Helps Us Separate the Noise From the Risk

One of the reasons we created the Income Method is that it helps us distinguish between events that we should react to and events that we don't need to react to. The Income Method emphasizes focusing on the income an investment is producing, rather than the share price.

This is a key point because there are many news items that might impact an investment's trading price but are not likely to threaten its income. Some people misinterpret our focus on income as an assertion that income never changes. This is false.

Dividends do change. Our portfolio has dividend raises and cuts every single year. Companies default on interest payments. Corporate bankruptcies happen every year. When you follow the Income Method and have a portfolio of 42+ income-producing investments, which we call The Rule of 42, you will see your income change every quarter. You will see dividend raises and cuts every single year.

Focusing on income helps us differentiate between issues that have a real impact on the bottom line and profitability of an investment and issues that are temporary and won't have a long-term impact on an investment.

When faced with a news item like a hurricane that causes substantial property damage, we can turn on the news and see the most devastating imagery. We can empathize with those whose lives are being dramatically disrupted and sadly lost in some cases.

While you're watching the news, you will eventually start to wonder, "What does this mean for my investments?"

Realty Income (O) owns 725 properties accounting for 3.6% of annual rent in Georgia, 493 properties in North Carolina, and 570 in Tennessee. In short, O likely owns over 1,000 properties that experienced some degree of impact from Hurricane Helene. Most REITs own many properties in the areas that were hit, as the "Sun Belt" has been a region highly favored by REITs due to its growth outlook.

What does this mean for O? It isn't like this is O's first hurricane. It has gone through Hurricane Katrina, Ian, Michael, Andrew, and numerous smaller storms and none of them interrupted the dividend payment for even a month.

The damage is real, the toll on the people impacted is certainly tragic and if you dive deep enough, you can calculate the financial impact. Yet, the reality is that for most publicly traded companies, the financial impact isn't large enough to materially alter their earnings. At the end of the day, earnings are what pay interest and dividends.

You will find that with many of these events, the financial impact is nowhere close to large enough to plausibly put the dividend at risk. When that is the case, income investors can simply look at any trading-induced dip as a buying opportunity.

Occasionally, events are large enough to put a dividend at risk. For example, Hawaiian Electric Industries (HE) suspended its dividend following the wildfires in Maui, as it faced substantial cap-ex needs and potential liability for numerous lawsuits.

Identifying When To Dig Deeper

As income investors, our focus needs to be to estimate the impact that a particular event will have on earnings and determine if that puts the dividend at risk. If it does, that is when we want to consider our future with that investment. If it doesn't, we can comfortably hold through whatever price volatility the market produces.

Instead of asking whether an event is "good" or "bad" for a company, ask yourself, "Does this event put earnings under significant enough pressure to put the dividend at risk?" Losing buildings to a hurricane or flooding is never a good thing. It is a cost to REITs like O, and that cost is primarily reflected in insurance costs, as these buildings are always insured.

If you really want to grow a pointy head and bury yourself in spreadsheets, I'm sure you could come to a rough dollar figure for how much Hurricane Helene will impact REITs through higher insurance costs in the future. Yet, at the end of the day, you would be putting in a lot of effort for a result that doesn't mean much. Your analysis can begin and end with, "is the damage from Hurricane Helene significant enough to have a material impact on earnings and threaten the dividend?" In this case, No, it isn't.

So often, I see investors go down the rabbit hole trying to calculate the precise impact of something that can clearly be dismissed as immaterial on its face. Attempting to account for every penny across the 42+ companies you invest in is something that is neither possible nor practical. Fortunately, it isn't necessary. By focusing on the income, we can bring ourselves back to the surface so that we don't get lost in the minutiae.

With each issue, we can ask ourselves whether it is plausible that a particular issue might threaten the dividend. If the answer is "maybe," that is when we want to put effort into digging deeper and doing calculations. We can keep going until either the answer is "no, this won't impact the dividend", or until we decide it is too uncertain, and we can get safer income elsewhere.

A lot of things will happen that are "bad" for a particular company. Every company in the world is always facing challenges that they have to overcome. That is simply part of running a business. Those of you who have run your own businesses are familiar with the never-ending list of "problems" that you had to solve. As fast as you solve one, three more crop up. By focusing on the income, we can take a step back and only spend our valuable time on the problems that are potentially big enough to impact our bottom line.

Bear Markets & Recession

If you are part of our investing group, you know I'm in the camp that believes a recession is likely coming soon and that will almost certainly mean a "bear market". Some have suggested that the upcoming recession might be among the most severe we have ever seen in the United States. I disagree, I think the upcoming recession is most likely to be quite mild from a fundamental standpoint. However, life is uncertain, and I could certainly be wrong.

What if those who believe that we might see a bear market that lasts 10+ years are right? Does that change my investment strategy? Should I run to cash and sit on my piles of cash, waiting for Armageddon?

No.

We invest in companies that produce cash flow and pay that cash flow out to investors in the form of interest and dividends. Is that cash flow guaranteed? No. It isn't guaranteed in any environment; as noted above, we see changes in our portfolio every single year. During a black swan, we will see more dividend cuts than usual, and more companies than usual will default on their interest payments.

We saw this during COVID-19, which was far worse than most recessions are on dividend payments. Companies that had perfectly "safe" dividends in January 2020 were cutting/suspending their dividends in March. Did it nullify the Income Method? No.

Members who followed our plan found that their income had substantially, if not fully, recovered by the end of the year. Through 2021 and 2022, they saw their income growing at a much faster pace than usual. The reason is that even in events that influence almost everything, not every company is equally impacted. Everyone in the world was impacted by the pandemic, but even during COVID-19, many companies continued raising their dividends. Investors who focused on the income were able to find opportunities to sell those who were suspending their dividends and reinvest into investments that had much safer income.

Our investment strategy accounts for the risk of periods with more dividend cuts than usual. The Income Method has several protective measures.

  1. Diversification. We follow The Rule of 42, which emphasizes owning at least 42 different income-producing investments. Even during periods of elevated dividend cuts, not every investment in every sector is going to be cut at the same time. Companies don't cut dividends because a recession or bear market is happening. Companies cut dividends because their earnings are expected to decline to a level where management believes paying the current dividend is unwise. In every recession, there are always certain segments of the economy that bear the brunt of the impact and other segments that aren't hit so hard. A recession is not uniform throughout the economy, and frequently, the sectors hit worst in the prior recession are not the same as those that will be impacted the most in the next one. By being diversified, we can ensure that only a portion of our portfolio is impacted significantly enough that dividends are cut.
  2. A high allocation to fixed-income. Even in the segments that are hit the hardest by recessions, fixed-income investments are more stable. Common dividends are entirely voluntary and are the first to be reduced or eliminated. Preferred dividends are pre-determined and companies are only going to suspend them when the issues are very serious. Interest payments are required, and if a company fails to pay them, they are going to go bankrupt more often than not. As a result, a portfolio of fixed income is likely to see much more stable income even in a very severe recession.
  3. Reinvesting at least 25% of your income. A large part of the Income Method is to ensure that you have a cushion. Based on our experience and backtesting, planning on reinvesting 25% is enough to ensure that your withdrawals will remain covered even through periods where dividend cuts are high.

These guidelines will help ensure that you have cash flowing into your brokerage even during the worst periods. What happens when you have cash flowing into your brokerage when prices are super low because the market is in a panic? It means you can buy opportunities that are producing cash flow at much higher than normal yields.

"Focus on the income" is not a statement that says, ignore reality and just buy anything that currently has cash flow. It means that we focus on buying companies with a lot of cash flow and are able and willing to pass that cash flow along to investors. We don't panic over oscillations in market prices. We aren't trying to predict world events and trying to trade ahead of the crowd. We aren't trying to duck in and out of the market.

Instead, we look at each company, understand the business model, and do our best to predict whether that company will be able to continue paying us a generous dividend. Sometimes we will be wrong, that's investing. However, the guidelines outlined in the Income Method exist specifically to protect us from the reality that we will be wrong, some events will happen that we fail to predict, and some investments won't provide the cash flow we expected. That's ok. When you have a good, robust strategy, your portfolio can absorb mistakes, it can absorb you being wrong sometimes, and it can absorb recessions.

If your strategy relies on you always being right, on you being able to predict when prices will change, when bear markets will start, when the bottom is in, and when the next Black Swan is going to swim by – I wish you the best of luck. My strategy doesn't require me to be right all the time, which is a good thing because I'm not going to be right all the time.

When you reach the point where you have the confidence that your investment strategy is solid and that it can absorb your mistakes and the unforeseen, you will find yourself having a lot less fear, uncertainty, or doubt. While you might second guess specific positions, you will have the confidence that your overall portfolio is capable of surviving anything and that the strategy you have implemented will lead you to success.

The Income Method is that strategy for me and thousands of my closest friends.

data.title: Market Bargains: 3 Dividend Stocks With Double-Digit Return Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Have you made money so far this year?

Unless you're a very active trader (90/90/90 rule) or someone with a highly unique investment approach, odds are you have made some decent money this year.

Looking at the year-to-date performance of major futures, it's fair to assume that a lot of investors are doing quite well.

  • Gold is up 25%, fueled by an elevated deficit in a good economic environment, geopolitical concerns, and a Fed that started cutting rates with core inflation well above its target.
  • The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 are up 18% and 15%, respectively, supported by the FANG+ members. More recently, however, the market has been broadening, as the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) is also up 15%.
  • Even the German DAX is up 13%, as investors have shrugged off fears that Europe's biggest economy may be in secular decline.
  • Russell 2000 small-cap stocks have lagged the market. However, even these stocks are up 8%.
  • Crude oil has been a rollercoaster. However, even trading below $70, WTI is still up 6% year-to-date.
  • Bonds are flattish.

The bad news is that putting new money to work has become a lot more challenging. Looking at my own portfolio, there are not many stocks that are screaming buys right now. At the start of this year, I almost could have picked random stocks from my watchlist that would have had a great valuation. That's not the case anymore.

Even in August, valuations already became an issue, as James Mackintosh wrote in The Wall Street Journal that investors should be prepared to make portfolio adjustments:

However, even the longest-term thinkers should adjust their portfolios when prices are way out of line with reality (the dot-com bubble or the Covid bust, bonds at close to zero yield, the SPAC/cannabis/clean energy bubble of late 2020-21). The question is how to judge.

One of the charts used in the article shows a comparison of the S&P 500's forward price/earnings ratio and the ten-year total return. Data was collected between 1988 and 2014.

As we can clearly see below, the higher the valuation, the lower the expected future return. This makes sense, as it is much more attractive for new capital to flow into stocks when valuations are low.

I highlighted this issue in a recent article as well. That article also included the chart below. Essentially, it's the same chart as above, except that it shows the 3-year subsequent annualized return.

This is what Apollo Global Management, the producer of the chart, wrote:

Looking at the historical relationship between the S&P 500 forward P/E ratio and subsequent three-year returns in the benchmark index shows that the current forward P/E ratio at almost 22 implies a 3% annualized return over the coming three years, see chart below. In other words, when stocks are overvalued like they are today, investors should expect lower future returns.

The data from private equity giant Apollo is similar to the WSJ's chart, as it implies a forward annualized return of 3% over the next 3-10 years.

Goldman Sachs agrees with that as well, as it sees a baseline of 3% average annualized returns over the next ten years.

Using more Goldman Sachs data, this is what the annualized 10-year returns distribution since 1930 looks like:

With all of this in mind, I'm not selling anything. I haven't been a net seller since I started investing in dividend stocks. In fact, I cannot even tell you what it would take for me to start selling, excluding personal issues where I suddenly require a lot of cash.

My strategy continues to be buying undervalued dividend (growth) stocks at attractive prices. This year alone, I have added four new companies to my portfolio. All of these positions are currently profitable.

However, it is getting harder to find good picks. Most stocks on my watchlist are trading at unfavorable prices, which goes for a lot of companies on the market.

Hence, we need to be extremely careful when searching for undervalued stocks. If the market trades at a lofty valuation, a company that appears to be cheap may have hidden issues. After all, why would a company be cheap if the market isn't cheap?

In an environment where the market is cheap, it is somewhat easy to find good investments, as even high-quality companies are attractive. When the market is expensive, many low valuations could be red flags.

With that said, in the second part of this article, I will present three high-quality dividend stocks, including a low-yielding company with aggressive dividend growth potential and a company yielding 9%. I believe all of these companies have what it takes to beat the market on a longer-term basis.

So, let's start with the lowest-yielding stock.

Cheniere Energy, Inc. (LNG) - A Dividend Growth Gem In Energy

In-depth research --> (Article)

On October 17, I wrote an article about two undervalued dividend stocks. One of them was Cheniere Energy. I decided to include the company again, as I believe it does not get the attention it deserves and is a perfect fit for this article.

Cheniere Energy is, as the name already gives away, an energy stock. However, it does not produce oil and gas. Cheniere turns natural gas into liquified natural gas ("LNG"), which explains its ticker. LNG is then shipped to export markets like Europe and Asia.

LNG is essential because the United States cannot export natural gas to Europe and Asia through pipelines. After years of aggressive investments in infrastructure, the country became the biggest global LNG player last year. This is mainly due to Cheniere, which was the first company with operational mega facilities for LNG production.

The beauty of this business model is that on top of elevated secular growth, the company has highly favorable contract structures that erase natural gas price risks and allow the company to benefit from potential increases in global LNG prices.

We have contracted substantially all of our anticipated production capacity under SPAs, in which our customers are generally required to pay a fixed fee with respect to the contracted volumes irrespective of their election to cancel or suspend deliveries of LNG cargoes, and under IPM agreements, in which the gas producer sells natural gas to us on a global LNG or natural gas index price, less a fixed liquefaction fee, shipping and other costs. The SPAs also have a variable fee component, which is generally structured to cover the cost of natural gas purchases, transportation and liquefaction fuel consumed to produce LNG. - Cheniere 2023 10-K.

On a longer-term basis, we can expect demand to almost double in Asia, mainly supported by fast-growing markets like China and India. Through 2040, Asia is expected to account for 80% of demand growth.

As Asian markets are growing rapidly and looking to slowly but steadily replace coal with cleaner natural gas, American gas is perfect, as it has low-cost production basins like the Marcellus in Appalachia and Permian/Haynesville in Texas/New Mexico.

It also has a fantastic pipeline system, providing Cheniere with plenty of feedstock for LNG production.

With that said, unlike many younger companies, Cheniere Energy has profitable infrastructure in Corpus Christi and Sabine Pass. These assets provide enough free cash flow to support its dividend, buybacks, debt reduction, and investments in expansion projects like Corpus Christi Stage 3.

Although LNG yields just 1.0%, it aims to maintain 10% annual dividend growth until the mid-2020s. Once Corpus Christ Stage 3 is finished, it will accelerate both dividend growth and buybacks, as it aims for a 20% payout ratio. Currently, its payout ratio is close to 10%. This bodes very well for its dividend!

It also has a $4 billion buyback program through 2027. This translates to roughly 10% of its market cap.

Even better, by 2026, analysts expect annual free cash flow to rise from $2.0 billion (in 2024E) to $3.5 billion. This would imply a free cash flow yield of more than 8%, better than almost any non-commodity dividend stock on my radar.

In other words, the dividend is much more attractive than one might think.

The company also has a fantastic valuation.

Trading at a blended P/E ratio of 11.2x, analysts expect 10% EPS growth in both 2025 and 2026. Applying its long-term average P/E ratio of 21.3x implies a fair price target in the $260-$270 range, roughly 45% above the current price.

Especially if cyclical economic growth improves in Europe and Asia, I expect Cheniere's EPS growth to remain elevated.

Cheniere is part of one family account. As I wrote in prior articles, the plan is to add it to other accounts as well.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) - A Correction Opportunity

In-depth research --> (Article)

UnitedHealth is the 7th-largest holding of the Dow Jones. It is also one of the best dividend growth stocks in history, returning 652% over the past ten years alone.

However, despite beating recent earnings, its stock price sold off by 9%. This was triggered by fears of rising costs in the insurance industry, which hurt the company's guidance.

The giant health insurer’s stock plunged 9% Tuesday morning as investors worried that rising utilization of care in both the Medicare and Medicaid insurance books could continue to be a problem next year as well, hurting the company’s earnings growth. The worries were further exacerbated by a cut to the upper end of its previous adjusted-earnings guidance for 2024 as well as an initial guidance for next year that fell short of analyst consensus. - The Wall Street Journal

It also did not help that the company's medical-loss ratio jumped from 82.3% to 85.2%. Analysts were looking for 84.4%. This ratio shows the share of premiums spent on patient care. The higher, the less money UNH retains.

Hence, the company is working with states to make sure that the rates they get for patients who are left behind accurately match the costs. This is needed after many healthier patients were forced out of Medicaid during the pandemic.

In general, the company is moving into high-growth markets, as it expects to serve more than 2 million new commercial consumers and provide care for 4.7 million people through value-based care arrangements.

UnitedHealth's growth also extends to the pharmaceutical side, with Optum Rx handling more than 1.6 billion prescriptions.

Hence, despite growth issues, the company's long-term target of 13% to 16% EPS growth remains valid. Even with headwinds like the Inflation Reduction Act, CMS Medicare rate cuts, and inflation, the company sees a lot of value in value-based care and technology benefits in patient care and efficiency. This includes artificial intelligence, which could be highly beneficial for healthcare.

"Value-based care" is the company's focus on finding a better mix of affordability and care quality.

While I expect inflation and cost pressures to remain an issue in the insurance business, I still like the risk/reward of UnitedHealth. This includes its 1.5% dividend. This dividend has a payout ratio of 30% and a five-year CAGR of 15%.

Its most recent hike was 11.7% on June 7, which reflects its confidence in its long-term outlook. Analysts agree.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, the street expects 10% EPS growth in 2024, potentially followed by 9% and 14% in 2025 and 2026, respectively.

Applying the company's 10-year average P/E ratio of 20.2x, we get an annual total return outlook of 10-12%. This number could accelerate after 2026 if it manages to turn its 13-16% annual growth outlook into reality.

That said, I also believe that the potential election of President Trump could cause UNH to perform well, as the former president is expected to be favorable for healthcare and financial regulations.

As a result, despite headwinds, I believe UNH remains an attractive dividend growth stock.

Western Midstream Partners (WES) - An Energy Gem Yielding 9%

In-depth research --> (Article)

I like midstream companies. This may not come as a surprise. Before the pandemic, they were horrible investments. Now, they are among my favorite dividend stocks, as the industry benefits from mature (profitable) infrastructure, strong demand and supply tailwinds, a bigger focus on free cash flow, and investors' demand for high-quality income.

That's where Western Midstream comes in. This company is a Master Limited Partnership (which issues a K-1 form) with strong ties to energy giant Occidental Petroleum (OXY). Occidental owns 49.9% of the units and is responsible for roughly 60% of the company's revenues. This includes 86% of WES' crude oil and natural gas liquids throughput.

The company owns more than 14 thousand miles of pipelines, 21 gathering systems, 70 processing and treatment facilities, seven natural gas pipelines, and 12 crude oil/natural gas liquids pipelines. These assets are located in a number of top-tier basins, including the Delaware Basin (part of the Permian), the Powder River Basin, DJ Basin, and South Texas.

While slightly more than half of its revenue comes from the Delaware Basin, the basin gets 80% of its capital spending, which makes sense, as it's the fastest-growing basin in the United States. This year, WES expects to spend $0.66 of every CapEx dollar on expanding its footprint, which bodes well for future growth.

Almost all of its contracts are fee-based, which means investors have minimal commodity price exposure. This makes midstream a great way to buy energy income without having to check oil and gas prices every day.

It also provides a lot of capital to unitholders.

At the end of the second quarter, the company achieved its leverage target of 3x EBITDA (earlier than expected). This opens up new opportunities for capital allocation.

  • Organic Growth: WES will continue to invest in expanding its business through high-returning organic projects. This almost goes without saying, as the company operates in areas with elevated demand for new infrastructure.
  • M&A: WES is looking for accretive acquisition opportunities that could further improve its portfolio and operational capabilities.
  • Distribution Increases: Given the company's strong financial position and leverage target achievement, WES is evaluating the possibility of increasing its base distribution to unitholders.
  • Buybacks: In order to be more flexible when it comes to distributing additional cash, the company is using unit buybacks.

When it comes to its distribution, the company made a major move on April 19. Back then, it hiked its distribution by 52.2% to $0.875 per unit per quarter. This translates to a yield of 9.0%.

Next year, analysts expect the company to generate $1.5 billion in free cash flow, slightly more than 10% of its market cap. This protects the dividend, as it already includes capital spending plans.

In 2026, free cash flow is expected to rise to $1.6 billion, 11% of its current market cap.

The company also bought back 7% of its shares over the past three years.

Valuation-wise, Western Midstream trades at a blended P/OCF (operating cash flow) multiple of 7.5x, which is more than a full point below its normalized multiple of 8.7x.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect per-unit OCF to rise by 20% this year, potentially followed by 7% and 3% in 2025 and 2026, respectively.

This implies a fair unit price of roughly $51, 31% above the current price.

When including its distribution, I believe the company could return 15-20% per year, making it a great pick for deep-value income.

Takeaway

The takeaway here is straightforward: even in a challenging market, where finding undervalued stocks feels increasingly difficult, I'm sticking to my strategy, as I continue to prioritize quality dividend (growth) stocks with attractive long-term potential.

Cheniere Energy, UnitedHealth, and Western Midstream offer unique opportunities across different sectors, each with fantastic business models, strong fundamentals, and growth opportunities.

Whether it's Cheniere's wide-moat and aggressive free cash flow growth, UnitedHealth's proven resilience in healthcare, or Western Midstream's high-yield stability, I'm very confident these picks will continue to outperform the market in the long run while providing a steadily increasing income stream.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast: Up And Up
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up and up

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is getting shinier by the day. The price of the precious metal touched another record, maintaining its streak of successive all-time highs. Since the start of 2024, gold's value has risen by a third, hovering over the $2,700 per ounce mark in October. The simmering tensions in the Middle East and the uncertainty over the upcoming presidential elections in the U.S. have increased the demand for the safe haven asset.

Golden opportunity: 2024 gains have come to fruition after years of lackluster performance over the past decade. The price of the bullion touched about $1,900 in 2011 and had been trading at around the same price for much of 2023. After being subdued for so long, gold prices shot up, delivering a 70% jump in the past two years. What's more is that big banks see this rally continuing into 2025. Analysts at Citi raised their three-month forecast for gold prices to $2,800/oz and set a $3,000 forecast for the next 6–12 months. J.P. Morgan, too, has noted that gold's fundamental price drivers still appear to have upside.

Uncertainty fueling the rally? While the current geopolitical situation and U.S. elections are influencing gold prices, Citi's latest estimates note a possible further U.S. labor market deterioration and interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve raising bullion prices. On the demand side, central banks in emerging Asian economies have stepped up their purchases in recent years. Investors are also investing in gold, eyeing the growing government debt level in Western countries, as well as sanctions making nations wary of the U.S. dollar.

Boost to miners: Investor fervor into gold has also spilled into equities, with precious metal stocks hitting 52-week highs. This included Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Alamos Gold (AGI), ASA Gold (ASA), Barrick Gold (GOLD), DRDGold (DRD), Eldorado Gold (EGO), Endeavour Silver (EXK), among others. "Mining stocks typically outperform gold during bull markets, providing additional leverage and profit potential for investors in precious metals," said SA analyst Jon Wolfenbarger. "Rising costs for mining companies have been a key driver of why mining stocks have underperformed," commented SA analyst Jason Hamlin.

What else is happening...

Short interest shifts point to a post-earnings rally for General Motors (GM).

Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) gains after report activist pushing for breakup.

Biden, Harris propose free OTC contraception under Affordable Care Act.

Kansas City Fed's Schmid says he opposes aggressive rate cuts.

Disney (DIS) names Morgan Stanley's James Gorman as next chairman.

Top small-cap stock picks following the group's upgrade to neutral.

BHP, Vale (VALE) near $30B settlement in Brazil tailings dam disaster.

Nike (NKE) signs long-term extension with the NBA and WNBA.

US Steel (X) gains as local union voices support for Nippon Steel deal.

FOMO is making investors not take market risk into account.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -1.4%. Hong Kong +0.1%. China +0.5%. India -1.2%.

In Europe, at midday, London -0.8%. Paris -0.8%. Frankfurt -0.4%.

Futures at 7:00, Dow -0.4%. S&P -0.5%. Nasdaq -0.6%. Crude +1.1% to $70.78. Gold +0.5% to $2,752.20. Bitcoin -1.7% to $67,160.
