data.title: Vimeo: A Hidden Gem In Enterprise Video
data.description:
Introduction

Vimeo (NASDAQ:VMEO) is growing its enterprise video segment at 55% CAGR and is on track to achieve $100 million annualized run rate this year. The company is also improving its profitability with 78% gross margin and 16% operating margin, achieved through strict cost controls and organizational optimizations. Additionally, Vimeo has also a strong balance sheet, with $311 million in cash and no debt.

All these metrics are very encouraging but they are not enough to offset the declines in consumer segment revenues and change the negative investor sentiment about the stock. Vimeo faces competition in the consumer market from YouTube, which concerns the investors. We agree that the company deserves a lower valuation due to YouTube competition, but not to this extent. We believe that the market is underestimating the potential of enterprise video and excessively discounting the stock, leading to a significant undervaluation of the company.

Even with a very conservative valuation approach, where we assign $0 valuation to the consumer business and only value the Enterprise segment, we still see 140% upside potential from current price levels.

Enterprise Video is a Different Market

The most critical area for Vimeo's future growth is Enterprise Video. It's key to understand that Enterprise video has very different needs and requirements compared to consumer video market. This is where investors might miss the point as they may see enterprise video as an extension of consumer video.

Unlike entertainment-based video consumption, organizations use enterprise video to improve business capabilities, train their employees, coordinate processes and offer a corporate communications platform in order to make operations more seamless and efficient. They also adopt enterprise video to external activities such as marketing and sales in order to increase customer engagement and accelerate deal cycles.

​​The global enterprise video platform market size is projected to grow from $22 billion in 2024 to $76 billion by 2032 (17% CAGR). We believe Vimeo is very well positioned to capitalize on the growing enterprise video trend, thanks to their Vimeo Enterprise platform which addresses key organizational concerns such as scalability, management, compliance, and data privacy controls. We think that these requirements position Vimeo favorably in the enterprise video market.

Enterprise Segment Revenue is the Focus

Company posted revenue of $104 million which was above analyst estimates with an increase of 2% on a Y/Y basis. However, the company also issued full year guidance of $405 million in revenue which indicates a decline of 3% YoY. We believe that this revenue decline trend will continue few more quarters as the enterprise segment revenue is still relatively small compared to overall revenue and will not be able to offset the churn in the Self-service segment.

However, the total revenue is not our primary focus. The most important metrics to look for are the Enterprise segment revenue and bookings. Vimeo's enterprise segment had another very strong quarter and achieved $20 million in revenue (55% YoY). The record revenue was driven by the strong demand for secure and managed enterprise video platforms as well as new products such as AI powered video translation. The quarterly revenue trajectory for Enterprise Video shows a strong growth momentum as the upward trend continues. The improving retention rates suggest that revenue will remain at these elevated levels.

The other key metric to track is Enterprise ARPU which shows Vimeo's ability to grow its average deal sizes and increase sales within existing customer base. The quarterly ARPU numbers show a significant increase since FY 2023 Q4 and we believe this trend to continue. The company is deepening its relationship with enterprise customers and creating additional products and use cases that will attract other business units within these organizations.

Solid Operational Performance

Vimeo's operating metrics continue to improve. Free cash flow reached $20 million, up from $13 million a year ago (54% YoY). The gross margin came in at 78% for Q2, which was flat YoY. However, EBITDA margin improvement was very strong and ahead of expectations at 16% (vs 5% last year). Vimeo improved its operating margin significantly by reducing costs. The company reduced its advertising spend by 50% and sales and marketing expenses by 27% YoY. These margin gains clearly show the company is executing with strict financial discipline and focus on profitability.

For Q3 and Q4, company has provided adjusted EBITDA margin guidance of high single digit which is relatively low compared to recent margin achievements.

The reason for this margin drop is that the company is shifting into investment mode to accelerate its Enterprise business. See CFO commentary on the Q2 call:

​Gillian Munson (CFO):

So, as you look at the business in the coming quarters, it's going to look probably less efficient. That's what the guidance implies as well, but that's us really making a determined effort to invest in the business. So, I think the answer is sort of both. We think there's always more efficiency to get. We won't be happy until we get the kind of margins we think we deserve. However, in the near term, we are moving into more of an invest in the business mode because we think the best way to get shareholder returns out there, the kind of shareholder returns we really want is the combination of margin and growth.

Overall, we believe Vimeo is operating with disciple and is successfully controlling its margin strategy. The company wants to reaccelerate its growth profile while balancing profitability through disciplined operations.

Valuation of Vimeo Enterprise Business

Vimeo's enterprise value is about $510 million, with an estimated FY 2024 revenue of $405 million. This puts the company's EV/S valuation at roughly 1.2x sales (see below). The reason for this very low valuation is obviously its revenue decline and fierce YouTube competition in its consumer business. Given these factors, we want to assign a valuation of $0 valuation to Self-serve and Other business as they are expected to continue declining. Our focus is on valuing the Enterprise Video business, where we see the actual investment opportunity.​

Over the last three years, Enterprise business has grown at 55% CAGR. In the next three years, as Vimeo continues to invest in the Enterprise segment, we are expecting Vimeo's annual revenue to reach $267 million at a 45% 3-yr CAGR and maintain its high gross and operating margins, demonstrating strong profitability.

We are assigning a conservative forward sales multiple of 10, assuming the enterprise segment can maintain a 45% CAGR due to the continuous introduction of new enterprise video products. Applying a discount rate of 8.1%, we arrive at a price target of $12, indicating a 140% upside potential from current price levels (see below).

Note that this valuation only accounts for the Vimeo Enterprise segment. The Self-Serve and Other businesses are considered additional upside potential and are not included in this calculation.

Risks

The main risk Vimeo faces is obviously the competition from YouTube. Google has several advantages over Vimeo, including scale, resources, ecosystem plus it has a free product. Another risk is the decline in total revenue, which is expected to continue for several more quarters. The Enterprise segment is still relatively small and won't be able to offset the losses of the self-serve segment. There is risk that this can put sales pressure on the stock in the short term especially if the enterprise segment revenue growth shows any softness.

Conclusion

Vimeo is executing on its growth strategy. The company has a huge opportunity in the enterprise video segment, solid financials, and a very attractive valuation. However, investors are currently caught up in the negative sentiment due to YouTube competition and declining revenues. We think that Vimeo has a clear path to get out of this downward consumer spiral because the growth opportunity lies not in the consumer business but in the enterprise. Enterprise Video is growing at 55% CAGR and it will vastly improve Vimeo's value proposition.

Our assessment shows that the market is discounting the stock excessively leading to a significant undervaluation of the company. We believe the stock has a great risk-reward profile and our valuation of the Enterprise business suggests a 140% upside potential, with a target price of 12. We rate Vimeo as a Buy.

data.title: ASML: Don't Catch A Falling Knife (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) (OTCPK:ASMLF) recently made headlines as its stock crashed following a disastrous earnings outlook. From my technical analysis below, I determine that this could just be the beginning and the stock could have much further to fall before a bottom is in. In addition, when evaluating its valuation multiples, it is clear that ASML is overvalued relative to its current growth rate and future growth prospects. With China's export restrictions becoming increasingly strict, the outlook for ASML is becoming ever more uncertain. There is a famous saying that investors shouldn't catch a falling knife, and this may very well be one of those times. Therefore, I initiate ASML at a strong sell rating.

Daily Analysis

Chart Analysis

The daily chart is quite an ugly one for ASML. There is much more resistance overhead than there is support. The one support level identified is at 700, which was support very early this year, and it looks like it is currently providing support after the crash. The nearest resistance level is in the 770s, as that represents a former gap. Since support reverses to resistance once broken, there could be a source of resistance. The next level of resistance would be a downside gap in the 840s. The stock failed to truly break out from this level in September and earlier this month, and the stock paid the price. The next level of resistance is the downtrend line that began in July and is nearing 850 and dropping fast. In addition, the 940s zone seems to be significant as it was both support and resistance multiple times during the past year, and the 1060s is another gap. Lastly, as you can see, the uptrend line that stretches back to last November was broken in July, showing that the near-term uptrend was over even before the recent crash. Overall, I believe it is clear the short-term outlook for ASML is quite dismal, as there is a formation of a downtrend and plenty of resistance above.

Moving Average Analysis

The 50-day SMA and 200-day SMA formed a death cross back in September, and the gap between the two has been widening ever since, showing that bearish momentum is building. The stock is trading far beneath both MAs, showing it may be a bit oversold. For the Bollinger Bands, the stock is currently below the lower band, showing the extent of the recent crash. Although this signals that the stock is oversold, stocks can remain at the lower band for extended periods. The Bollinger Band's 20-day midline is the nearest MA and could act as resistance since the stock has now entered into a near-term downtrend. From my analysis, I believe ASML's stock is in quite a dire situation in the short term, despite being perhaps a bit oversold currently.

Indicator Analysis

The MACD line just very recently crossed beneath the signal line, which is a bearish signal. The gap between the two continues to widen significantly, demonstrating strong bearish momentum. The MACD also showed early trouble with the prior uptrend as the MACD failed to form new highs in July while the stock surpassed the high in March. For the RSI, despite the recent crash, the stock is not oversold. The RSI, similar to the MACD, also displayed negative divergence in the prior uptrend, signalling trouble. Lastly, for the stochastics, the %K line broke below the %D line just very recently and the gap between them is enormous as the %K line plunges. This again signals strong bearish momentum. In my view, these indicators show a worrying short-term outlook, as there are basically no positives to be taken from this analysis.

Takeaway

The chart, moving average, and indicator analyses converge to the conclusion that the short-term situation for ASML is rather dire with the outlook quite weak. The charts show a formation of a new downtrend with heavy overhead resistance, while the MAs show accelerating bearish momentum. Lastly, the indicators are flashing bearish signals and a further look back shows that the recent former uptrend was unconfirmed by key indicators.

Weekly Analysis

Chart Analysis

The intermediate-term chart is a better-looking one for ASML, as there is some support underneath. The stock recently broke a steeper uptrend line, but there is still a longer-term less steep uptrend line that shows the stock may still be in an intermediate uptrend. The nearest area of support would be at 700, as identified in the daily analysis. Here, you can see this level's significance stretches back multiple years, as it has been support and resistance multiple times. The next area of support would be in the 590s, as this area also has been support or resistance multiple times in the past years. Moving down, we have the uptrend line that I mentioned earlier and the last level of support would be at around 400 as that level was resistance in 2020 and support in 2022. As for resistance, the nearest level would be at 750 as that area was significant resistance in 2023. The former broken uptrend line could also be a source of resistance but is quickly moving out of range. Lastly, the 870s seems to be an important level as well. Overall, I believe that the intermediate-term chart is better than the short-term, but the stock is still far from the support of the key uptrend line, showing it could have much further to fall.

Moving Average Analysis

The 13-week SMA had a bearish crossover with the 26-week SMA earlier this year, and the stock has fallen since this signal. The gap between the two MAs is widening quickly, showing the acceleration of bearish momentum. Like in the daily analysis, the stock is trading far below both of the MAs. For the Bollinger Bands, the stock is at the lower band, showing it could be oversold currently. But again, stocks can remain oversold for extended periods. While the chart analysis above shows that we are still in a slower uptrend, the Bollinger Bands show it is a weak uptrend as the stock has failed to hold above the 20-week midline. Instead, the midline acted as resistance earlier this year, showing that if there is an uptrend, it is a weak one. In my view, the moving average analysis shows that nearer-term bearish momentum is rising while the intermediate-term uptrend is in question.

Indicator Analysis

The MACD crossed below the signal line in the middle of the year, and the stock has plunged since that signal. After briefly narrowing, the gap between the two has recently started to widen again, showing a resurgence of bearish momentum. For the RSI, like in the daily analysis, it is still not oversold despite the recent crash. The RSI also shows there was a negative divergence with the 2023-2024 uptrend. The RSI had a top failure swing as it recorded higher levels in 2021 than it did earlier this year, which should have served as an early warning sign the uptrend was unsustainable. Lastly, for the stochastics, the %K line just crossed below the %D line, another bearish signal, and is nearing the oversold 20 level. From my analysis, it is clear that not much is going right for ASML in the intermediate term.

Takeaway

While the weekly chart is less negative than the daily chart, the overall intermediate-term outlook for ASML is less than ideal. The chart shows that the key support of the uptrend line is far beneath the stock, while the MAs show increasing bearish momentum. The Bollinger Bands show that the uptrend is weak, and the indicators are all flashing bearish signals.

Monthly Analysis

Chart Analysis

Note that the above chart is on a logarithmic scale to better reflect the long-term situation of ASML. There is not too much to discuss here, but as you can see, the stock remains in a very long-term uptrend that stretches all the way to 2008. The stock has bounced off the line multiple times in the last decade, demonstrating its significance. However, the stock is currently still far above this support level, since this is a log scale. Currently, this line is at around 570, which is still miles below the current stock price. As we move into 2025, it could get interesting as even if the stock drifts horizontally out, this line could come into play. In addition, I have also identified a support area at around 400, which was also discussed in the weekly analysis. In my view, this chart shows that ASML is still indeed in a very long-term uptrend, but with support still far below, the stock may have room to fall further.

Moving Average Analysis

The 6-month SMA and the 10-month SMA just had a bearish crossover, flashing a sell signal. With the 10-month SMA still relatively flat and the 6-month SMA sharply declining, I would expect the gap to widen quickly as bearish momentum builds. The stock is trading quite far beneath both MAs. For the Bollinger Bands, the stock just crossed below the 20-month midline, showing the longer-term uptrend is weakening. Despite the recent plunge, the stock is nowhere near the lower band, showing it is not oversold in the longer time frame. I believe the above chart shows an extremely bearish situation for ASML, as the combination of the bearish crossover in the MAs and the breach of the Bollinger Band's midline indicates a nasty technical outlook for the stock.

Indicator Analysis

The MACD just crossed below the signal line very recently, flashing a bearish signal. In addition, the MACD shows there is a long-term negative divergence with the stock. The MACD peak in 2021 was much higher than the peak this year, and this signal should have served as a warning sign. As for the RSI, it has broken below the critical 50 level, showing the weakening of the long-term uptrend. The RSI also shows a negative divergence, as it failed to enter the 70 zone this year. Lastly, for the stochastics, the %K line broke below the %D line at around the 80 level earlier this year, another highly bearish signal. The gap between the two lines is widening significantly, indicating accelerating bearish momentum. Overall, I believe there is not much to like about ASML, even in the longer time frame, as all three key indicators are bearish.

Takeaway

While we are still in a very long-term uptrend, ASML's long-term outlook is still not very bright. The logarithmic monthly chart shows that the support of the uptrend line remains far below the stock, while the MAs and Bollinger Bands show a concerning outlook. As for the indicators, all three of them are highly bearish, converging to the conclusion that the technical picture is dismal.

Fundamentals & Valuation

ASML reported its latest earnings on October 15th, and it was quite the event, to say the least. First off, the report was released early due to a technical error. They reported revenue of $8.13 billion or 7.5 billion euros, which beat expectations by $326.87 million. As for net income, they reported 2.1 billion euros, representing an EPS of $5.75. It is a 15.66% increase YoY, and it beat expectations by $0.46. While these results were obviously respectable, the main story that crashed the stock was the dismal guidance figures they provided. They projected that they will have between 30 and 35 billion euros of net sales in 2025, which is in the lower half of the previously provided guidance range. This is mainly the result of ASML having to deal with the U.S. and Netherlands' export restrictions to China. This could significantly affect ASML's future growth prospects. Other highlights in their earnings would be the gross margin of 50.8% and net bookings of 2.6 billion euros in the quarter.

Although the P/E and P/FCF ratios have receded from the highs set earlier this year, they remain elevated, especially the P/FCF ratio. The P/E ratio currently sits in the mid-30s, after almost touching 60 earlier in the year. However, this is still significantly higher than the lows of 2022 of around 25. For the P/FCF ratio, it is currently at 90, down from above 150 earlier in the year. It remains highly elevated compared to levels seen in the past few years, as the ratio hit a low of below 25 in 2022. As you can see in the revenue and net income growth chart above, the current growth rate for ASML is very similar to the one seen in the middle of 2022. In addition, new export restrictions could further dampen ASML's growth as we head into 2025. Since current growth is similar to 2022 and the growth outlook is concerning, I believe ASML is overvalued at current levels. In 2022, there was arguably a better outlook, with the AI trend just getting underway and export restrictions less stringent. The current valuation multiples are higher now than they were back then. Seeking Alpha currently has a D valuation rating for ASML, confirming my evaluation.

Conclusion

The pullback in ASML stock may be just getting started, as indicated by both the technical and fundamental analyses above. Technical signals from all three time frames show a negative outlook for ASML, as moving averages show that the bearish momentum is accelerating. Although the stock remains in an intermediate and long-term uptrend, there are signs these trends are lacking in strength and support remains far below the stock's current levels. In addition, after giving dismal 2025 guidance, it is clear the stock is overvalued as valuation multiples remain elevated relative to ASML's growth rate. Therefore, investors of all time frames should not try to catch the falling knife and instead stand aside and wait for the stock to stabilize before attempting to jump in. Since the technicals and fundamentals converge to the conclusion that the stock could have much further to fall, I initiate ASML at a strong sell rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cameco: My Stock Pick For This Decade, Here Is My Half-Decade Report
data.description:
Investment thesis

At the start of 2020, I wrote an article picking Cameco (NYSE:CCJ) (TSX:CCO:CA) as the stock that could be among the best long-term performers for the decade. It was trading at just under $9/share at that point. My most recent addition to my Cameco stock position was in early September when it dropped below $40/share. After the recent run-up in its stock price that brought its forward P/E ratio to over 90, I have been contemplating taking profits. However, it increasingly looks like the market is finally going all-in in terms of bullish sentiment on uranium, based on expectations of a new global nuclear power renaissance. Such periods of market enthusiasm do have a way of surpassing fundamental limits, so for now, I am holding my position, looking for a better opportunity to take profits, even though Cameco's valuation is getting to be increasingly hard to defend through fundamentals-based arguments.

Switching From A Buy To A Hold

Before we analyze the internal aspects of Cameco and the relevant external factors, I want to briefly address and clarify the reasoning behind my shift from a buy the last time I covered Cameco over a year ago, to a hold right now. Since I wrote that article, this stock has gone up over 100%, and at this point, I feel that the valuation is getting too far ahead of its otherwise solid revenues and earnings growth prospects. I by no means see a worsening trend in its overall performance or a deterioration of the uranium market on the horizon.

Cameco's Prospects For Production, Revenue, And Profit Growth, Vs. Its Runaway Valuation

For the latest quarter, Cameco saw an increase of 24% in revenues compared with the same quarter from a year ago. Revenues came in at $598 million. Its profits more than doubled for the same period from $14 million in Q2, 2023 to $36 million for the latest quarter. The outlook for the year suggests Cameco is currently trading at roughly eight times its expected revenues for the year.

Uranium production surged by 61% compared with the same quarter from last year. Some of the uranium produced contributed to an increase in inventories, which acts as a drag on earnings since money was spent on operations that resulted in higher mined extraction than sales. In other words, Cameco's current profitability potential is better than the latest quarterly numbers suggest.

One arguably negative trend observed in the last quarterly report was the decline in its cash position of just over $200 million. Its total debt declined by $300 million, and the value of inventories increased, which signals its net financial health is improving.

Interest on long-term debt reached $28.9 million, which amounts to 4.8% of revenues. I tend to worry once this ratio surpasses 5% because an excessive debt servicing burden can eventually hurt a company's earnings, and other aspects of its financial performance & health.

Production, Earnings & Revenue Growth, Vs. Valuation

Cameco is seeing robust growth in production & revenues, which arguably makes it a growth stock; therefore, it makes some sense for it to be trading as a growth stock.

As we can see, Cameco currently has a forward P/E ratio of over 90 which is significantly higher than many of the popular tech stocks that tend to dominate the finance & investment news cycle. For this valuation level to make sense, there has to be a credible argument in favor of robust growth expectations going forward.

An important factor to consider in this regard is whether Cameco has decent production growth prospects. There are many projects where Cameco can either increase production or start new mining operations. The Millennium Project in Saskatchewan is one such opportunity to significantly increase production if the market signals warrant the full development of the mine. It is estimated that it has 53 million pounds of indicated resources in place, as well as 20 million pounds of inferred resources. Based on these resources, Cameco could increase production by about 7 million pounds per year, on average, once production commences.

The Yeelirrie Project in Australia has 128 million pounds of measured and indicated resources. It is unclear at what pace of production Cameco will produce those resources, once it decides to bring them online, but at the moment it does not see a need to commence development. The Kintyre project in Australia is in the same standby status phase, and it has almost 60 million pounds in indicated & inferred resources.

Between these three projects that are on standby, Cameco can bring an extra 250 million pounds of uranium or more to the market. The current value of these standby resources based on the uranium spot price is about $20.8 billion. Assuming a 10-year mine life on average, these mines could hypothetically provide an extra $2 billion/year in revenues if they were to be brought online simultaneously. With all else held equal, earnings would double as well.

While Cameco's standby projects have the potential to increase production significantly, perhaps double its current output, it does not seem to be nearly enough to justify its current market cap. Uranium prices, and by extension Cameco's realized price, need to rise significantly in order to help it catch up to its valuation.

Uranium Market Prospects

The spot price roughly quadrupled since I called the bottom of the uranium market almost a decade ago. It may be tempting to assume that after such a dramatic increase in uranium prices, there is very little upside left.

The one aspect of nuclear power that needs to be understood is that, unlike most other commodities, its consumption dynamics are not as easily affected by price swings. Nuclear reactors need fuel, and the cost of fuel in the total cost of nuclear power production makes up a relatively small percentage of the total cost. For instance, The Nuclear Innovation Alliance estimates that raising the cost of uranium ore from $30/lb to $130/lb will increase the cost of producing nuclear power by about 1/6.

Higher uranium prices will not cause nuclear power plants to shut down. It does not prevent new ones from being built, especially the ones that are already under construction. The four-fold increase in uranium prices since the bottom certainly doesn't seem to curb the growing enthusiasm for nuclear power.

The latest positive trend seems to be a push to use small nuclear reactors to power AI data centers. More broadly, utilities and national governments have an eye on nuclear power as a solution for dealing with issues such as reducing emissions or dealing with hydrocarbon fuel shortages. For instance, nuclear power seems to be gaining traction in the EU after it ended its dependence on Russian natural gas. Italy is the latest nation to pursue new nuclear reactors. Not long ago, it was thought that nuclear power had no future in the EU, given pressures to shut down existing reactors.

Several factors, ranging from environmental concerns associated with burning coal, to supply growth constraints for the oil & gas industry due to a lack of new conventional field discoveries so far this century are all having a positive impact on non-hydrocarbon energy demand growth. The electrification of the global economy is estimated to lead to an approximate doubling of electricity demand by 2050 from current levels.

Initially, it was hoped that wind & solar power would replace current hydrocarbon electricity generation, as well as nuclear power, which is also opposed by environmental groups.

A very little noticed and discussed turning point came about in 2021, when the EU, which is a leader in wind & solar power generation suffered through a serious energy crisis, due to the intermittent nature of renewable energy. Germany, for instance, saw a 13% decline in onshore wind power generation in 2021, compared with the previous year. Other countries experienced a shortfall as well, which led to a massive increase in natural gas demand to compensate for the shortfall, thus a massive price spike. It is not entirely well known and understood that the EU's natural gas price crisis started in the fall of 2021 before the Ukraine war became a full-blown confrontation in 2022.

The discussion that needs to be had around the subject of energy security within the context of the push for intermittent energy sources to dominate the grid is not a pleasant one, which is why the discussion is not taking place in the public sphere. It does seem that at government policy and private sector levels, the lessons of the 2021 energy crisis in the EU were learned, even if with some delay. There are currently 59 new reactors under construction, in addition to the 441 reactors that are currently operational. Four of the reactors currently under construction are in the EU. Based on Italy's latest signals, we will likely have significantly more nuclear reactors under construction in the EU within a few years. Other countries around the world are also looking at nuclear power as an increasingly desirable solution to their energy needs.

There are still about 750 million people who lack electrical power in their homes, while the world's population is growing by tens of millions of people every year, which means that potentially hundreds of millions of new electricity consumers are going to be added every year going forward. At the same time, the global personal transport system is becoming more electrified. AI data centers are increasing their energy use at a rate of about 20% per year.

These are all factors that are set to increase electricity consumption per capita, even as the number of consumers of electricity is rising as well. Adding it all up, electricity demand growth, which is already forecast to be robust, has the potential to surprise on the up-side. Environmental & hydrocarbon availability issues are likely to put a strain on the world's ability to meet that demand. We are also coming to terms with the limits of wind & solar. As a result, nuclear power demand may surge around the world. With the prospect of small reactors coming online within a few years, which have the potential to scale up nuclear power capacity much faster, as well as old reactors having their life extended, global uranium demand may surge within a few years beyond some of the most optimistic expectations.

Investment Implications

After the sizable increase in its stock price, and considering its high P/E ratio, ordinarily, this would be a stock I would be selling. I first bought Cameco stock in 2017, and I traded it semi-actively along the way, reducing my position after significant stock price upswings while adding more after significant dips. I intend to continue doing this for the foreseeable future. I believe that the uranium market is just in the early phases of a prolonged bull run. Most of the long-term fundamentals are going in its favor. I intend to take profits again once its stock price rises above $60. Then I will probably add more shares to my portfolio in response to the next significant dip in its stock price. For now, the stock is a hold from my perspective. It had a good run, and the market is currently bullish on the entire sector. Actively trading it on the way up, taking some profits after new record highs, while buying the dips can help to enhance returns, which is needed going forward because an almost 600% increase in its stock price that we saw in the first half of the decade is unlikely to be repeated in the second half, no matter how positive the outlook for the uranium market happens to be going forward.
